query: Transfusion Related Acute Lung Injury symptoms and pathophysiology

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In the US, TRALI has been responsible for at least 30% of transfusion-related deaths. While the mortality rates have dropped over the past decade, continued awareness is key.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify diagnostic criteria for transfusion-related acute lung injury. Describe the pathophysiology of transfusion-related acute lung injury. Contrast transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI) and transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO). Review the importance of improving care coordination amongst interprofessional team members to improve outcomes for patients with transfusion-related acute lung injury. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the US, TRALI has to be reported to the Blood Banking services.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: TRALI can develop within 6 hours to 72 hours of blood transfusions that are rich in plasma. Despite suspected various hypothesis, it could certainly be prevented with a careful approach in blood transfusions, especially in those who are more vulnerable for acquiring TRALI. Due to no definitive treatment, prevention is the best key to keep TRALI from occurring by treating underlying diseases first.

retrieved_docs.title: Massive Transfusion -- Complications -- Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury
retrieved_docs.content: TRALI occurs when the recipient's neutrophils become primed to activate due to the patient's underlying condition. These primed neutrophils subsequently become activated by the antibodies targeting HLA or the biological response modifiers. Once activated, these neutrophils release cytokines, reactive oxygen species, oxidases, and proteases that damage the pulmonary capillary endothelium. The incidence of TRALI rises in direct proportion to the quantity of blood products administered.

retrieved_docs.title: Massive Transfusion -- Complications -- Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury
retrieved_docs.content: Massive transfusion can cause transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI), a rapid-onset lung injury leading to non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema. [32] [33] TRALI type I manifests in patients without additional risk factors for acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS). However, TRALI type II occurs in patients with ARDS risk factors or those who already have ARDS but experience acute decompensation due to a transfusion. Symptoms of TRALI typically present within 1 to 2 hours of the transfusion, but can potentially be delayed up to 6 hours. Approximately 85% of cases result from the infusion of antibodies targeting human leukocyte antigen (HLA) or antineutrophil antibodies present in the donor blood products. The remaining 15% likely originates from biological response modifiers, such as soluble CD40 ligand, within the stored blood product.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Despite aggressive support, mortality rates of more than 12% are reported once TRALI is diagnosed. Even those who survive have a slow recovery.

retrieved_docs.title: Platelet Transfusion -- Complications -- Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury
retrieved_docs.content: Transfusion-related acute lung injury is a life-threatening complication that presents as acute lung injury with hypoxemia (PaO 2 /FiO 2 ≤300 mm Hg or oxygen saturation <90% on room air) during transfusion or within 6 hours of cessation with no evidence of acute lung injury before the therapy. [31] Radiographic evidence comprises bilateral lung infiltrates without circulatory overload.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Immediate management of TRALI is to stop the transfusion and notify the blood bank to screen the donor unit for antileukocyte antibodies, anti-HLA or anti-neutrophil-specific antibodies. [5] Supportive measures must be taken to improve oxygenation. Although there is no specific treatment for TRALI, low tidal volume application is feasible in this case as TRALI's pathophysiology is similar to acute lung distress syndrome (ARDS). Best practice is prevention. In the United Kingdom, the incidence of TRALI was substantially reduced by using plasma from male donors and screening female donors for HLA and HNA antibodies which are strong risk factors. Once the transfusion is stopped, gradual recovery may take 2-4 days. The chest x-ray will improve within 2-5 days.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI) is a clinical syndrome in which there is an acute, noncardiogenic pulmonary edema associated with hypoxia that occurs during or after a transfusion. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of transfusion-related acute lung injury and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Histopathology consists of early acute lung distress syndrome (ARDS), revealing interstitial and intra-alveolar edema and the presence of neutrophils in the interstitial and airway. Lung sections have shown numerous neutrophils in pulmonary capillaries and small pulmonary vessels. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: True TRALI should not have any risk factors for acute lung injury according to diagnostic criteria. TRALI is caused by damage to pulmonary vasculature from neutrophil-mediated in forms of human neutrophil antigen (HNA) or human leukocyte antigen (HLA) antibodies in donor blood which bind to antigens of a recipient. Storage of blood products can accumulate proinflammatory mediators that can cause TRALI as well. A two-hit hypothesis applies in this clinical syndrome. Neutrophil sequestration occurs in the pulmonary vasculature, and neutrophils activate to damage the endothelial layer, causing leakage of protein and fluid into alveolar space. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians must stop the transfusion immediately and administer supplemental oxygen. A trial of continuous positive or bilevel airway pressure may be helpful, but 70% to 80% of affected patients will require endotracheal intubation. In addition, hemodynamic support with fluid resuscitation or pressors may be necessary. Clinicians should use diuretics with caution, as their use may result in hypotension in patients who are hemodynamically stable. Patients who survive TRALI can receive blood transfusions in the future, but they should not receive transfusions from the same donor. Strategies to help reduce the incidence of TRALI include the following: Exclude plasma donations from high-risk donors, such as multiparous women Screen and defer donors with HLA antibodies Deferral of donors implicated in a specific TRALI case Selection of male donors or female donors who have never been pregnant for high plasma volume components

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion Reactions -- Pathophysiology -- Acute Transfusion Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: Transfusion-related acute lung injury: Acute lung injury is due to antibodies in the donor product (human leukocyte antigen or human neutrophil antigen) reacting with antigens in the recipient. The recipient’s immune system responds and causes the release of mediators that lead to pulmonary edema. Possibly contributing to this are clinical conditions that predispose the patient including infection, recent surgery, or inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: TRALI was first reported in the 1950s but recognized as a distinctive clinical syndrome in 1983. [3] The disorder is not only diagnosed clinically but is usually confirmed by radiographic findings. Diagnostic criteria for TRALI is if the symptoms develop during or within 6 hours of transfusion without any risk factors for developing acute lung injuries such as sepsis from pneumonia, aspiration, and shock. [4] Physical symptoms include fever, hypotension, and tachycardia. Clinical findings include exudative bilateral infiltrates on chest radiograph, no evidence of pulmonary vascular overload, and hypoxemia of SpO2 less than 90% on room air with a ratio of the partial pressure of oxygen to a fractional inspired oxygen concentration of less than 300 mmHg. [5] [6] Possible TRALI is when there are other risk factors for acute lung injury. Delayed TRALI is when transfusion is completed after 6 to 72 hours, and it is associated with higher mortality. [5] [7] Transfusion-related circulatory overload (TACO) needs to be ruled out as it can be on differential diagnosis due to the similarity of pulmonary edema picture, but due to actual volume overload. [2] [3] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2792
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is classified as a specific type of lung disorder (اِضطِراب الرِّئَة), indicating that it falls under the broader category of pulmonary disturbances. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians understand that respiratory distress syndrome shares fundamental characteristics with other lung disorders, which can guide accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment strategies, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific form of فقر الدم (anemia) that arises rapidly after substantial blood loss, leading to a sudden decrease in circulating red blood cells and hemoglobin levels. As a subtype of anemia, it shares the fundamental characteristic of reduced oxygen‑carrying capacity of the blood, but its acute onset distinguishes it from chronic or nutritional anemias. Prompt recognition and treatment of acute posthemorrhagic anemia are essential in patient care, as rapid restoration of intravascular volume and red cell mass—through fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion, and control of the bleeding source—are required to prevent tissue hypoxia, organ dysfunction, and hemodynamic instability. Understanding that acute posthemorrhagic anemia falls under the broader category of فقر الدم helps clinicians classify the condition, anticipate its pathophysiology, and apply appropriate therapeutic strategies within the overall framework of anemia management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 813
retrieved_docs.content: Internal injury of chest is recognized as a traumatic event and functions as a cause of subsequent medical complications, highlighting its role as an initiating injury in clinical assessments and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 862
retrieved_docs.content: دبيلة is associated with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse, indicating that individuals with دبيلة may develop these respiratory complications. Recognizing this relationship is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and appropriate management of patients presenting with دبيلة.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2246
retrieved_docs.content: Tromethamin is categorized within the therapeutic class of Blood Products/Modifiers/Volume Expanders, indicating that it functions as a volume‑expanding agent used to restore or maintain intravascular fluid volume. This classification reflects its role in clinical situations such as hemorrhage, hypovolemia, or other conditions requiring rapid hemodynamic support, where augmenting plasma volume is essential. Recognizing tromethamin as part of the Blood Products/Modifiers/Volume Expanders class helps clinicians select appropriate volume‑expanding therapies and understand its mechanism of modifying blood components to increase circulatory volume.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 861
retrieved_docs.content: Internal injury of chest is identified as a traumatic event and is noted as a cause_of subsequent medical complications. This specific injury has parent crushing injury or internal injury, indicating that it belongs to the broader category of crushing or internal injuries. Additionally, crushing injury or internal injury is associated_with إصابة, emphasizing the direct link between these mechanisms of injury and the resulting tissue damage. Recognizing that internal injury of chest functions as a traumatic event, is classified under crushing/internal injuries, and is associated with injury (إصابة) aids clinicians in understanding causality, assessing severity, and delivering appropriate care for patients with thoracic trauma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2888
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with anemia (فقر الدم), as the sudden loss of renal function often leads to reduced production of erythropoietin and impaired clearance of uremic toxins, which together contribute to a decrease in red blood cell count. This relationship highlights the importance of monitoring hemoglobin levels in patients with acute renal injury and addressing anemia promptly to improve oxygen delivery, reduce cardiovascular strain, and support overall recovery. Effective management of both conditions is essential for optimizing patient outcomes in the setting of acute kidney dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2812
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, which is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, is a hereditary hemoglobin disorder that can precipitate serious respiratory complications in adults. Clinically, داء الكريات المنجلية is associated with respiratory failure, respiratory insufficiency, and adult cardiac arrest, underscoring the need for vigilant respiratory monitoring and prompt intervention in patients affected by this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 1186
retrieved_docs.content: Internal injury of chest is categorized as a type of crushing injury or internal injury, indicating that it has the broader parent classification of crushing injury or internal injury. As a traumatic event, an internal injury of chest can act as a cause of subsequent medical complications. The occurrence of this chest injury is frequently described as being “due to” and therefore is associated with crushing mechanisms or other internal injury processes. Understanding that internal injury of chest is both a traumatic event that can be a cause of further injury and a subcategory within crushing injury or internal injury helps clinicians assess its etiology, anticipate related complications, and guide appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: are organized within injury classification frameworks, share common external cause codes, and often involve similar pathological outcomes such as tissue damage and neurotoxicity, providing a cohesive framework for assessment and management of traumatic patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1995
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydration (التجفاف) is clinically associated with the development of acute kidney injury (فشل كلوي حاد). In turn, acute kidney injury is clinically linked to low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), reflecting the importance of adequate intravascular volume in preserving renal function. Acute kidney injury also encompasses the condition of extracellular blood nitrogen (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية), indicating that disturbances in nitrogenous waste handling outside the kidneys are a relevant component of its clinical spectrum. Understanding these interconnections—how dehydration can precipitate acute kidney injury, how hypovolemia contributes to its progression, and how extracellular nitrogen abnormalities manifest—helps clinicians identify risk factors, monitor patients, and implement timely interventions to protect renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is closely linked to داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي, an anti‑basement membrane disease of the kidney, and it also forms a key component of متلازمة رئية كلوية, a pulmonary‑renal syndrome that involves simultaneous injury to the lungs and kidneys. This interrelationship highlights the importance of recognizing necrotizing glomerulonephritis not only as a severe renal pathology but also as a manifestation of systemic autoimmune processes that can affect multiple organ systems, guiding comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and coordinated therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 161
retrieved_docs.content: underlying tissue‑damage morphology. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how traumatic events—whether burns, firearm exposures, vehicle or aircraft crashes, or internal chest injuries—are systematically categorized, coded, and linked to common pathological outcomes such as tissue damage and potential neurotoxicity, providing a coherent framework for injury documentation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ, indicating that a general loss of biological function can be linked to hepatorenal failure—a severe condition in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it highlights the need to assess and manage systemic organ failure comprehensively in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1248
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is classified under the broader category of اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (renal glomerular disease). It is closely associated with داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي (anti‑basement membrane disease), reflecting an immunologic mechanism that targets the glomerular basement membrane. Clinically, necrotizing glomerulonephritis often occurs within متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome), linking severe renal inflammation with pulmonary hemorrhage. Understanding these interconnections is essential for accurate diagnosis and coordinated management of patients who present with combined kidney and lung involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1027
retrieved_docs.content: unspecified state of consciousness is associated with إصابة داخلية (internal injury), reinforcing the connection between severe intracranial bleeding and internal traumatic damage. Together, these relationships map a comprehensive hierarchy of general to specific injuries, illustrating how broad injury categories subdivide into distinct anatomical and pathological entities that are relevant for diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.
